step2_rag_chunks: Smoking is often tied to a habit loop â€” a cue triggers the urge, smoking is the routine, and relief is the reward. Understanding what your specific cues are is the first step. It could be a time of day, a place, an emotion, or even a person. Once we identify the cue, we can work on changing just the routine while keeping the relief.

A craving is not a command â€” it is a wave. It builds, peaks, and fades, usually within 3 to 5 minutes. Urge surfing means observing the craving without acting on it â€” noticing where you feel it in your body, watching it get stronger, and then watching it pass. Each time you surf an urge without smoking, it gets slightly easier the next time.

Bidi smoke is more concentrated than cigarette smoke and delivers higher levels of nicotine despite being thinner. This means bidi dependence can be as strong as or stronger than cigarette dependence, and withdrawal symptoms can be equally intense. Standard cessation support â€” behavioral strategies and nicotine replacement â€” is just as applicable and effective for bidi smokers.

step2_rag_chunks: When a craving hits, delaying the response by even 5 to 10 minutes is often enough for it to pass. Tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to â€” and then do something else for those 10 minutes. Most of the time the urge has weakened significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up.

After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.
Talking to someone about your cravings can help reduce the intensity of the urge.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: The patient has signaled they are ready to act or is wrapping up. Your ONLY job is to give ONE warm closing sentence. Do NOT ask any question. Do NOT suggest anything new. Do NOT summarize what was discussed. Just acknowledge their readiness warmly and wish them well. Example: 'That sounds like a solid plan — take it one step at a time.' One sentence only. Nothing more.
